title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Credit Prices Strengthen, All RIN Prices Rise, and Type 1 Transfers Decline WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(05th January 2026 - 11th January 2026)
  • A total of 660,586 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a decline of 52.56% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades increased 16.72% WoW and a total of 2,511,986 non-zero credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $56.77/MT for Type 1 transfers, up by 4.47% WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers rose by 3.10%, price stood at $55.91/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 5.67% WoW; on 13th January, the spot price stood at $59.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $63.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 2.1% WoW, while D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 5.83% and 5.88% WoW. D6 RIN prices rose by 4.85% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, in a letter sent to congressional leadership, more than 70 biofuel and agricultural groups urged Congress to pass permanent year-round E15 legislation, arguing it would boost demand for a record 2025 corn harvest, support farm incomes, and deliver fuel savings for drivers. The groups emphasized that farmers cannot afford another season of uncertainty, and that markets need consistency and predictability that only permanent congressional action on year-round E15 can provide.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_610117" align="alignnone" width="663"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer price increased by 4.47% WoW, rising from $54.34 to $56.77, while all non-zero transfer prices moved up from $54.23 to $55.91, a 3.10% WoW gain. Type 1 trades totaled 660,586 credits, reflecting a 52.56% WoW decline and total non-zero transaction volumes rose by 16.72% WoW, reaching 2,511,986 credits for the week. [caption id="attachment_610118" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_610119" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 5.67% WoW, reaching $59.05/MT. On 13th January, the spot price stood at $59.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2026, stood at $63.00/MT on 13th January.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_610120" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, a total of 160 trades took place in the market, including 21 high-volume transactions. The highest reported transfer price was $159.00 for a Type 2 transaction involving 12,500 credits. The lowest transfer price was $47.50 for a Type 2 transaction covering 5,000 credits. The largest trade by volume was a Type 2 transaction of 300,000 credits priced at $53.57, while the smallest was a Type 1 transfer of 18 credits priced at $57.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_610121" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 spot price stood at $56.62 in January so far, rising from $53.08 in December. As of 10th January 2026, trade volumes for the month reached 2.7M.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_610122" align="alignnone" width="1300"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 5th January 2026 â€“ 11th January 2026, total VPT rose by 6.77% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 2.1% WoW, while D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 5.83% and 5.88% WoW. D6 RIN prices rose by 4.85% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Draft Rule Language for the Washington Clean Vehicles Program Rule Available for Public Comment: Department of Ecology is proposing updates to Washingtonâ€™s Clean Vehicles Program to stay aligned with Californiaâ€™s emissions standards, including amendments to Advanced Clean Cars, Advanced Clean Cars II, Advanced Clean Trucks, and the Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus programs. The agency is also considering adopting parts of Californiaâ€™s Truck and Bus rule and adding new fleet reporting requirements to ensure consistent, cleaner vehicle standards, support compliance, and advance Washingtonâ€™s greenhouse gas reduction goals. Public comments are open from January 6 to February 2, 2026.
  • Ag, Biofuel Groups Continue Call for Year-Round Sales of Lower-Cost E15: Last week, in a letter sent to congressional leadership, more than 70 biofuel and agricultural groups urged Congress to pass permanent year-round E15 legislation, arguing it would boost demand for a record 2025 corn harvest, support farm incomes, and deliver fuel savings for drivers. The groups emphasized that farmers cannot afford another season of uncertainty, and that markets need consistency and predictability that only permanent congressional action on year-round E15 can provide.
  • In 2025, U.S. Retail Gasoline Prices Decreased for Third Consecutive Year: The U.S. retail price for regular grade gasoline averaged $3.10 per gallon (gal) in 2025, $0.21/gal less than in 2024. This year marks the third consecutive year of declining nominal retail gasoline prices, according to data from Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update.
  • Clean Fuels Asks EPA to Maintain RFS Compliance Deadline: Clean Fuels Alliance America urged EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin to keep the 2025 RFS compliance deadline at March 31, 2026, warning that automatic delays caused by late 2026 RVO finalization would deepen the economic strain already facing biomass-based diesel producers and farmers. The group said ongoing rulemaking delays are disrupting feedstock contracts, production plans, and investment decisions, and noted that biodiesel output has already slowed without finalized volumes. They emphasized that timely completion of the 2026 rules is essential to protect farm income, support rural investment, and deliver the energy security benefits promised under the RFS.
  • California Plans $200 Million EV Rebate Program After End of Federal Tax Credits: California Governor Gavin Newsom has proposed a $200 million rebate initiative to support electricâ€‘vehicle purchases after the federal government ended the $7,500 new EV tax credit and the $4,000 used EV credit. The state aims to revive incentives that previously operated under the Clean Vehicle Rebate Program, which concluded in 2023 after assisting hundreds of thousands of buyers. EV sales declined sharply following the federal programâ€™s expiration. Automakers are also adjusting plans, with Stellantis ending certain plugâ€‘in hybrid models and General Motors absorbing large financial charges related to EV investments.
